topicArea: Cliché, Proverb & Knowing When the Moment Needs Common Words

questions.passage:
At the reception after the funeral, a neighbor takes the widow's hand and says that time heals all wounds. The sentence lands like a pebble dropped into a well, small, worn, and inadequate, and the widow thanks her while privately noting how little the words touched. Two years later, tidying the same kitchen, she catches herself thinking that the wound has, in fact, healed the way the phrase promised, and the discovery embarrasses her slightly. The cliché led a double life all along: dead in the mouth, alive in the world. That doubleness is worth more attention than the phrase ever gets.

The word itself remembers the printing house. A cliché was a stereotype plate, a phrase cast whole in metal because compositors set it so often, ready to be stamped without assembling the letters, and the technology is the meaning: a cliché is language pre-fabricated, reached for as a unit, no assembly required. Inside most of them a metaphor is sleeping, the ship of state, the flood of tears, figures so worn that no one sees the picture anymore. And each was once a coinage vivid enough to be repeated, which is the form's quiet tragedy: every cliché is a former success, killed by its own popularity, promoted from invention to furniture.

The case against the furniture is serious. The ready-made phrase does not merely decorate thought; it can replace it, the cast unit stamping itself where a fresh sentence would have required fresh seeing, and a mind that reaches for the nearest phrase has often stopped looking at the thing itself. Political language runs on exactly this convenience, prefabricated units, collateral damage, tough choices, doing quiet ideological work by sparing everyone the assembly, and the writing teacher's old war on cliché is at bottom a moral campaign: borrowed words, the charge runs, carry borrowed feeling, and the writer who deploys them has outsourced her own attention.

The proverb complicates the indictment. Sayings that survive generations of use are not merely worn; they are tested, compressed verdicts that stayed portable because they kept being true enough to carry, tools rather than ornament. The standing objection, that proverbs contradict each other, look before you leap against he who hesitates is lost, mistakes the genre. Proverbs are not universal laws but situation-indexed instruments, each filed under the circumstance it fits, and the wisdom was never inside any single saying. It lies in the diagnosis, knowing which situation one is in, and therefore which tool the moment takes.

Formula also has a social employment that novelty cannot fill. Weddings, funerals, and farewells run on expected words, and the expectation is the function: the common phrase enacts membership, saying what everyone says because everyone says it, the speaker stepping into a role the community built for exactly this doorway. The condolence line is the proof by counterexample. Original grief-writing from a near-stranger draws attention to its own performance, the mourner made audience to the speaker's eloquence, while the worn phrase, offered plainly, points away from the speaker entirely, which at certain thresholds is precisely the courtesy.

The formula fails, when it fails, by sealing what it should open. Everything happens for a reason, delivered into fresh grief, does not comfort so much as adjourn, a thought-terminating phrase closing a conversation the mourner may have needed, and the line between comforting commonality and evasive formula is real even where it is fine: the common phrase should stand beside the griever, not answer for the grief. Writers, meanwhile, have their own remedy for the dead metaphor, which is to wake it, literalizing the sleeping picture until the ship of state actually founders on something, renewal by re-seeing.

So the widow's embarrassment deserves revising. The phrase that failed at the reception was not false; it was early, and its wearing-out was the evidence of its truth, sentences do not get repeated for generations by being wrong. Clichés are where a culture stores what it has learned too thoroughly to keep saying freshly, and the storage is a service, not a scandal. The judgment that matters is timing: some moments need the new sentence, assembled with fresh attention, and some need the old one, offered like bread, and fluency in a language includes knowing which moment is which.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clichés are worn former successes that store tested cultural knowledge; the real skill is judging when a moment needs common words and when it needs new ones.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because so many well-known proverbs flatly contradict one another, traditional folk sayings encode no genuine practical wisdom and survive on rhythm and sheer memorability alone.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The writing teacher's war on cliché has failed because political language will always run on prefabricated units.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Original condolences are superior to worn phrases because they demonstrate genuine attention to the mourner.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage traces the cliché from stereotype plate through the serious case against prefabricated thought, rehabilitates the proverb as situation-indexed tool and formula as social employment, marks where formula fails by sealing what it should open, and closes on the widow's revision: the worn phrase was early, not false, and fluency includes knowing which moment needs the old sentence and which the new.
explanations.B: This choice adopts the standing objection the fourth paragraph answers. The contradictions mistake the genre, the passage argues: proverbs are situation-indexed instruments, and the wisdom lies in diagnosing which circumstance one is in, not inside any single saying.
explanations.C: This choice converts one exhibit into a verdict on a campaign. Political language's convenience illustrates the case against the ready-made; the passage neither declares the teacher's war lost nor makes politics the argument's center, and its own conclusion assigns formula legitimate work.
explanations.D: This choice reverses the fifth paragraph's counterexample. Original grief-writing from a near-stranger draws attention to its own performance, the mourner made audience; the worn phrase points away from the speaker, which at certain thresholds is precisely the courtesy.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fifth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: concede that ritual speech at weddings and funerals reflects the guests' lack of imagination.

choices.label: B
choices.text: establish formula's social employment, the expected phrase enacting membership, with the condolence line as proof that novelty can fail.

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that eulogies should be standardized to spare mourners the burden of performance.

choices.label: D
choices.text: introduce the thought-terminating condolence phrases and sealing formulas that the sixth paragraph goes on to criticize in considerable detail.

explanations.A: This choice reads the paragraph's account as an indictment it explicitly reverses. Ritual speech is not failed original speech; the expectation is the function, and saying what everyone says enacts membership rather than exposing imaginative poverty.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph opens by naming the employment novelty cannot fill, weddings, funerals, and farewells running on expected words, explains the mechanism, the common phrase enacting membership, the speaker stepping into a role built for the doorway, and closes with the counterexample: original grief-writing makes the mourner an audience, while the worn phrase points away from the speaker, the courtesy of commonality demonstrated.
explanations.C: This choice extracts a standardization policy from an analysis of function. The paragraph explains why expected words work at thresholds; it prescribes nothing about eulogies, whose composition it never discusses.
explanations.D: This choice assigns the paragraph the sixth's material. Everything happens for a reason and the sealing formula arrive afterward as the failure mode; the fifth paragraph's business is the success mode, formula serving where novelty performs.

questions.stem: The passage's account of the cliché as originally a printer's stereotype plate, in the second paragraph, chiefly serves to convey that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: printing technology dramatically accelerated the spread of worn stock phrases across national literatures and languages.

choices.label: B
choices.text: compositors were the first writing teachers to campaign against formulaic language.

choices.label: C
choices.text: clichés originated in journalism, where deadline pressure rewarded ready-made sentences.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the technology is the meaning: a cliché is language pre-fabricated, cast whole and reached for as a single unit.

explanations.A: This choice converts an etymology into a diffusion history. The paragraph traces the word's origin to explain what a cliché is, not how phrases traveled; circulation across literatures is nowhere discussed.
explanations.B: This choice invents an occupational irony the passage never states. Compositors cast the plates because they set the phrases often, the practice that named the object; the teacher's war belongs to the third paragraph and to a different profession.
explanations.C: This choice substitutes a plausible-sounding origin for the stated one. The plate was cast in the printing house generally, and the paragraph derives the concept from the technology's logic, not from journalism's deadlines, which go unmentioned.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph draws the equation explicitly: a phrase cast whole in metal, ready to be stamped without assembling the letters, and the technology is the meaning, language pre-fabricated, reached for as a unit, no assembly required. The etymology supplies the passage's working definition, the cast unit that the third paragraph then shows stamping itself where fresh seeing was needed.

questions.stem: According to the fourth paragraph, the defense of proverbs holds that their mutual contradictions:

choices.label: A
choices.text: are rare, since most proverbs address circumstances no other proverb touches.

choices.label: B
choices.text: show that proverbs were composed as jokes rather than as guidance.

choices.label: C
choices.text: prove decisively that traditional folk wisdom cannot withstand even the most elementary forms of sustained logical scrutiny.

choices.label: D
choices.text: mistake the genre: proverbs are situation-indexed instruments, the wisdom lying in diagnosing which circumstance one is in.

explanations.A: This choice defends proverbs by denying the phenomenon. The paragraph concedes the contradictions with its own example pair, look before you leap against he who hesitates is lost, and answers by reinterpreting them, not by calling them rare.
explanations.B: This choice invents a comic genealogy the paragraph never offers. Proverbs are presented as tested, compressed verdicts, tools that stayed portable by staying true enough to carry; humor as their origin appears nowhere.
explanations.C: This choice states the objection the paragraph exists to answer. That the contradictions discredit folk wisdom is the standing charge; the defense's whole content is that the charge mistakes the genre.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph gives the defense in full: proverbs are not universal laws but situation-indexed instruments, each filed under the circumstance it fits, and the wisdom was never inside any single saying, lying instead in the diagnosis, knowing which situation one is in, and therefore which tool the moment takes. Contradiction between tools built for different jobs is no flaw at all.

questions.stem: A young man is mocked by a friend for offering the standard my deepest condolences at a coworker's funeral instead of something more personal. Based on the fifth paragraph, the author would most likely respond that the young man:

choices.label: A
choices.text: deserves the criticism, since borrowed words carry borrowed feeling and outsourced attention.

choices.label: B
choices.text: chose safely but suboptimally, since a fresh personal sentence would have required, and publicly demonstrated, genuinely fresh seeing.

choices.label: C
choices.text: spoke fluently: at that threshold the common phrase enacts membership and points away from the speaker, the courtesy of commonality.

choices.label: D
choices.text: should have said nothing, since the sixth paragraph shows condolence formulas sealing what they should open.

explanations.A: This choice applies the third paragraph's charge outside its jurisdiction. Borrowed feeling indicts the writer who outsources attention where fresh seeing was the task; the fifth paragraph carves out exactly these thresholds, where the expectation is the function and commonality the point.
explanations.B: This choice imports the writing teacher's standard into the funeral line. Fresh seeing governs composition; at the doorway of grief, the paragraph holds that novelty from a near-stranger performs, and the safe phrase is not the lesser option but the fitted one.
explanations.C: Correct. The fifth paragraph analyzes the case in advance: funerals run on expected words, the common phrase enacts membership, the speaker stepping into a role the community built for exactly this doorway, and original grief-writing from a near-stranger draws attention to its own performance, making the mourner an audience. The worn phrase points away from the speaker, which at certain thresholds is precisely the courtesy; a coworker is that near-stranger, and the standard line that fluency.
explanations.D: This choice misapplies the failure mode. The sixth paragraph's sealing formula answers for the grief, everything happens for a reason adjourning a needed conversation; my deepest condolences stands beside the griever and closes nothing, the distinction the passage draws finely but firmly.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the fourth paragraph's claim that proverbs are tested verdicts that survived by being true enough to carry?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that many of the best-known common proverbs exist in nearly identical forms across several entirely unrelated languages and language families.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Studies finding proverb survival tracking rhyme, brevity, and vividness, the least accurate sayings persisting as robustly as the most accurate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that dictionaries of proverbs disagree about the origins of many sayings.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that younger speakers can complete fewer traditional proverbs than older speakers.

explanations.A: This choice supports the claim if it does anything. Independent survival of the same verdict across unrelated languages is what repeated testing against common human circumstances would produce; convergence is the tested-tool account's friendliest evidence.
explanations.B: Correct. The claim is selectionist: sayings stayed portable because they kept being true enough to carry. Survival tracking rhyme, brevity, and vividness instead, with the least accurate persisting as robustly as the most accurate, would replace truth-testing with catchiness as the filter, the verdicts surviving as earworms rather than as tools, the mechanism of the claim removed.
explanations.C: This choice concerns provenance scholarship, which the claim never invokes; a verdict can be tested across generations of use whatever the uncertainty about who first coined it.
explanations.D: This choice documents generational attrition in the stock, which bears on transmission today, not on why the survivors survived; a tested tool can still fall out of circulation when its circumstances thin.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the third paragraph's claim that the ready-made phrase can replace thought rather than merely decorate it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that readers rate essays containing familiar phrases as easier to read.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that professional speechwriters keep extensive files of reliable, field-tested stock phrases carefully organized by occasion and anticipated audience.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Experiments finding writers seeded with stock openers generated fewer novel observations, examined material less closely, and missed details controls caught.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Surveys finding that most people admit to using clichés when tired.

explanations.A: This choice measures reading ease, which is the prefabricated unit doing its advertised job for the audience; fluency for readers says nothing about whether the phrase displaced the writer's looking, the claim's actual mechanism.
explanations.B: This choice documents the inventory, not its cognitive price. Organized stock files show the plates exist and are reached for; the claim needs evidence that reaching for them stops the fresh seeing, which filing cabinets cannot supply.
explanations.C: Correct. The claim's mechanism is displacement, the cast unit stamping itself where a fresh sentence would have required fresh seeing, a mind that reaches for the nearest phrase has often stopped looking. Seeded writers producing fewer novel observations, examining less closely, and missing details controls caught is the stopped looking measured directly, and the deficit scaling with each supplied formula ties the blindness to the plates dose by dose.
explanations.D: This choice records self-reported conditions of use, fatigue, which is compatible with the phrase as harmless shortcut; when people reach for clichés is not evidence about what the reaching costs their attention.
